introduction: As MediConn Solutions continues to expand its reach, it is crucial to consider the unique needs of our Indigenous patients living in low-connectivity environments. This presentation explores potential compliance controls to ensure secure authentication and patient verification.

subsections.heading: Current Authentication Measures: An Overview
subsections.content:
• In FY2025, our efforts towards strengthening secure authentication protocols received a budgetary allocation of 9% from the total compliance and cybersecurity budget.
• This allocation translated to a significant investment of approximately $1.2M CAD, primarily focused on advancing the authentication infrastructure.
• These efforts underscore our commitment to meet and exceed regulatory expectations for secure digital identity verification, particularly for Indigenous patients.
• However, our investment in authentication controls may not be sufficient to keep pace with the evolving regulatory landscape, potentially impacting our ability to ensure secure patient verification.
• Looking ahead, we need to reassess our budget allocation for FY2026, with a focus on enhancing the security of our authentication systems for patients in low-connectivity environments.
• This strategic reallocation of resources will be crucial to uphold our commitment to providing reliable and secure remote healthcare services to all our patients, regardless of their location.
subsections.justification: This content underscores the need for higher investment in authentication controls to meet regulatory expectations, addressing the DR Question.

subsections.heading: Challenges in Low-Connectivity Environments
subsections.content:
• Internet connectivity issues can disrupt video consultations
• Poor connectivity can prevent uploads of patient data in real-time
• Real-time monitoring of patient's health can be challenging
• Limitations in bandwidth make it difficult to use high-resolution imaging
• Patients' digital literacy might be low in these environments
• Limited access to technical support can make troubleshooting difficult
• Dependence on mobile network data can increase costs for patients
subsections.justification: The content identifies general issues in low-connectivity environments but does not focus on compliance controls or patient verification.

subsections.heading: Proposed Compliance Controls for Q2 2026
subsections.content:
• Introduction of machine learning algorithms to detect fraudulent activities
• Implementation of a blockchain-based patient data management system
• Regular penetration testing to identify and fix potential security vulnerabilities
• Automated compliance reporting to keep track of all activities
• Implementation of GDPR-compliant data storage and transfer protocols
• Increase in employee training on new compliance measures and regulations
• Introduction of a dedicated compliance team to handle all related issues
subsections.justification: The content provides general compliance controls but does not address specific needs of indigenous patients in low-connectivity environments.

conclusion: By implementing these proposed compliance controls, we can enhance the user experience for Indigenous patients, even in areas with low connectivity. Let's approach Q2 2026 with a firm commitment to inclusive, secure healthcare solutions.
